_meta.source: Prime Self Sacred Texts & Canonical Philosophy
_meta.description: The Seven Knowledges — intellectual and spiritual foundations upon which all Prime practice rests
_meta.note: Six domain Knowledges (outward-pointing disciplines) plus the 7th Knowledge: Self (the meta-knowledge that makes all others coherent). These are not subjects to be studied once — they are ongoing disciplines to be lived. Together they form a complete map of self, relationship, and world.

self.essence: The practitioner who knows themselves cannot be surprised by themselves in battle.
self.description: The study of one's own identity — strengths, limitations, values, motivations, desires. Without this knowledge, all other power is directionless. This is the foundation upon which all Prime Self practice rests. Before you can invoke the wisdom of others, you must first understand your own design.
practicalApplication: Maintain a self-observation journal without judgment
Track when you feel most alive and most drained
Notice patterns in conflicts — what triggers you consistently
Distinguish between authentic desires and inherited expectations
Honor your Energy Blueprint Strategy and Authority in daily decisions
self.primeQuestion: Who am I when no one is watching and nothing is at stake?

fiction: The Stranger by Albert Camus
The Bell Jar by Sylvia Plath
Steppenwolf by Hermann Hesse
nonFiction: The Untethered Soul by Michael Singer
Awareness by Anthony de Mello
The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown

ancestors.essence: To know your ancestors is to multiply your intelligence across generations.
ancestors.description: Understanding one's cultural heritage, lineage, and family history. The wisdom of those who came before is not lost — it is encoded and waiting to be accessed. This knowledge forms the foundation of Priming: the ability to invoke ancestral wisdom for present challenges.
practicalApplication: Research family history — interview living elders
Map generational patterns (careers, relationships, health)
Identify inherited gifts you carry forward
Recognize inherited wounds requiring healing
Create ancestral altars or practices of remembrance
Study the historical figures who share your Forge and invoke their wisdom
ancestors.primeQuestion: What wisdom did my ancestors earn that I can inherit through deliberate practice?

fiction: Roots by Alex Haley
Homegoing by Yaa Gyasi
The Joy Luck Club by Amy Tan
nonFiction: It Didn't Start with You by Mark Wolynn
Women Who Run with the Wolves by Clarissa Pinkola Estés
Of Water and the Spirit by Malidoma Somé

theOne.essence: Compassion is not weakness — it is the highest form of intelligence, because it sees reality as it is.
theOne.description: The recognition that all living beings and the universe itself are deeply interconnected. Every action ripples. Every relationship is a mirror. This knowledge transcends the illusion of separation and reveals the unified field in which all consciousness participates.
practicalApplication: Practice seeing others as reflections of yourself
Notice ripple effects of your actions through systems
Cultivate compassion meditation practices
Recognize projection — what you judge in others exists in you
Develop ecological thinking in all decisions
Practice Clustering with aligned individuals
theOne.primeQuestion: How is what I see in others a reflection of what exists in me?

fiction: The Overstory by Richard Powers
Ishmael by Daniel Quinn
Cloud Atlas by David Mitchell
nonFiction: A New Earth by Eckhart Tolle
The Web of Life by Fritjof Capra
Braiding Sweetgrass by Robin Wall Kimmerer

constructive.essence: These are not rigidly 'good' traits — they are contextually appropriate behaviors chosen with consciousness.
constructive.description: The identification and cultivation of behaviors that build, improve, and create positive change. The Prime practitioner knows when to build. This knowledge encompasses virtues, but frames them as strategic choices rather than moral absolutes.

constructiveTraits.courage: Acting despite fear when values demand it
constructiveTraits.patience: Allowing processes to unfold in their natural timing
constructiveTraits.generosity: Sharing resources, knowledge, and energy freely
constructiveTraits.integrity: Alignment between values, words, and actions
constructiveTraits.curiosity: Approaching the unknown with openness rather than fear
constructiveTraits.compassion: Responding to suffering with presence and care
constructiveTraits.humility: Accurate self-assessment without inflation or deflation

practicalApplication: Audit which constructive behaviors come naturally vs. require cultivation
Practice one constructive trait deliberately each month
Notice when constructive behavior becomes rigid or performative
Balance construction with necessary destruction
Build what only you can build
constructive.primeQuestion: What constructive action is mine to take in this moment?

fiction: A Man Called Ove by Fredrik Backman
The Secret Garden by Frances Hodgson Burnett
East of Eden by John Steinbeck
nonFiction: How to Win Friends and Influence People by Dale Carnegie
Give and Take by Adam Grant
Atomic Habits by James Clear

destructive.essence: Destruction is not inherently negative — sometimes things must be torn down for growth to occur.
destructive.description: Equally essential to Constructive knowledge. The Prime practitioner understands when deconstruction serves the greater good, and applies it with precision, not impulse. Traits carry no permanent moral charge — context determines appropriateness.

destructiveTraits.ruthlessness: Cutting away what must go without sentiment
destructiveTraits.aggression: Directed force when boundaries are violated
destructiveTraits.coldness: Emotional detachment when warmth would enable dysfunction
destructiveTraits.rejection: Refusing what does not belong in your life
destructiveTraits.judgment: Discerning what is false, harmful, or misaligned
destructiveTraits.stubbornness: Holding position when others pressure compromise
destructiveTraits.wrath: Righteous anger that fuels protective action

destructive.contextualWisdom: Every destructive behavior has a constructive shadow. Ruthlessness without compassion becomes cruelty. Aggression without restraint becomes violence. The master knows WHEN destruction serves life and WHEN it merely serves ego.
practicalApplication: Audit what in your life needs to end
Practice the clean cut — ending without drama or negotiation
Distinguish destructive impulse from destructive wisdom
Accept that creation requires destruction
Release guilt about necessary endings
destructive.primeQuestion: What must I end or destroy so that something better can emerge?

fiction: Blood Meridian by Cormac McCarthy
The Road by Cormac McCarthy
Lord of the Flies by William Golding
nonFiction: The Prince by Niccolò Machiavelli
The 48 Laws of Power by Robert Greene
Antifragile by Nassim Nicholas Taleb

healing.essence: No Prime can be fully reached while carrying unprocessed wounds.
healing.description: The practice of mending — internal wounds, broken relationships, inherited damage. This knowledge includes the disciplines of forgiveness, reconciliation, self-acceptance, and making amends. Healing is not weakness; it is the foundation of sustainable power.

healingDisciplines.forgiveness: Releasing the demand that the past be different
healingDisciplines.selfAcceptance: Embracing all parts of yourself without condition
healingDisciplines.griefWork: Allowing loss to move through you completely
healingDisciplines.makingAmends: Taking responsibility for harm you have caused

practicalApplication: Identify your core wound — the recurring pain pattern
Practice self-compassion before extending to others
Make amends where possible without re-traumatizing
Distinguish forgiveness from reconciliation
Seek healing modalities that match your design
healing.primeQuestion: What wound am I carrying that, if healed, would liberate my full power?

fiction: A Little Life by Hanya Yanagihara
The Kite Runner by Khaled Hosseini
The Color Purple by Alice Walker
nonFiction: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
When Things Fall Apart by Pema Chödrön
Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach

selfMeta.essence: The awareness that recognizes all other Knowledges — the Heptad's overflow into self-awareness.
selfMeta.description: Distinct from Knowledge #1 (Self as the study of one's own identity — a discipline directed at the self as object). The 7th Knowledge is Self at the meta-level: the awareness that recognizes HOW you know, not just WHAT you know. It is the Knowledge of knowing itself — the person who knows their native epistemology, how their Authority shapes the way they receive and act on information, how their body/mind/field registers truth before it can be argued with. Without the 7th Knowledge, all other Knowledges are ungrounded: Sciences without Self produces brilliant blind spots. Arts without Self produces beautiful noise. Connections without Self produces codependence.
selfMeta.distinctionFromKnowledge1: Knowledge #1 (Self) is a discipline: study your identity, strengths, limitations. Knowledge #7 (Self Meta) is the structural completion: the awareness that makes all six disciplines coherent by knowing how you know. #1 looks AT the self. #7 looks THROUGH the self to the point where inside and outside cease to be the relevant distinction.
domains: Native epistemology — how your Authority registers truth
Cognitive architecture — how your Design processes information
Meta-awareness — the capacity to witness your own knowing
Integration — holding all six Knowledges as aspects of one intelligence
Self-referential completion — the system recognizing itself as a system

relevantGates: Gate 64 (Before Completion — holding the whole before it resolves)
Gate 1 (Self-Expression — the creative act of being)
centers: Ajna Center (how you conceptualize your own knowing)

primaryIndicators: North Node (becoming what you already are)
houses: 12th House (self as dissolution back to source)

practicalApplication: Name your Authority type as your native epistemology — the specific way truth registers in your body/mind/field before it can be argued with
Observe which Knowledge domains (Sciences, Arts, Defenses, Heresies, Connections, Mysteries) you default to — and which you avoid
Notice the difference between knowing something and knowing that you know it
Practice recognizing when you are IN a Knowledge domain vs. observing FROM the 7th Knowledge
The synthesis itself is a 7th Knowledge exercise — reading it with recognition rather than judgment
selfMeta.primeQuestion: How do I know what I know — and what does my way of knowing reveal about who I am?
selfMeta.convergence: The 7th Knowledge (Self as domain of intelligence) and the 6th Forge (Self as mode of operation) converge. They are the same moment viewed from two angles: the knowledge of how you work IS the forge of recognizing yourself. The Major Seventh resolving to the octave.
selfMeta.harmonicPosition: The Heptad position — the completed cycle that overflows its own container. The 7th Knowledge is the point at which the system of Knowledges becomes aware of itself as a system. Ti resolving to Do.

fiction: Gödel, Escher, Bach by Douglas Hofstadter
The Glass Bead Game by Hermann Hesse
Solaris by Stanislaw Lem
nonFiction: I Am a Strange Loop by Douglas Hofstadter
The Tree of Knowledge by Maturana & Varela
Who Am I? by Ramana Maharshi
